# Six in the Room, a Hundred in the Field

*A piece written for an onbuilding meeting — a working gathering where Movemental sits down with movement leaders not to pitch them, but to read their work back to them and ask what could be built across it.*

**In the room:**

- **Brad Brisco** — co-host
- **Alan Hirsch** — guest
- **Bree Mills**, **JR Woodward**, **Deb Hirsch**, and **Andrew Jones** — four movement leaders

---

## What this gathering is

Most rooms like this run on introductions. People say their name, their organization, the title of their latest book, and everyone nods at the parts they recognize. The work stays where it lives — in the books, the blogs, the talks, the networks each person carries on their own back.

This room is trying to do something different. Before anyone speaks, their work has already been read — all of it, carefully, the way you'd read a friend's whole catalog and not just the famous title. So the gathering can start where most gatherings never get to: not "who are you," but "here is what you've spent your life saying, and here is who else in this room has been saying something next to it without knowing."

That's the small experiment. Six people, decades of work between them, and a question: if the connections were already drawn, what would the room do with them?

And behind the small experiment is a larger one. Six is a number you can hold in your head. A hundred is not. So this piece moves from the six in the room to the hundred in the field, and tries to show what changes when the work of a whole movement can be read at once.

---

## The six, one at a time

### Brad Brisco

Brad Brisco has spent his career translating. He takes the language of post-Christendom mission — which can sound like seminary — and turns it into something a bivocational planter in Kansas City can actually use on a Tuesday. His core move is a reframe most people miss: he stopped saying *bivocational* and started saying *covocational*, because "bi" makes the day job sound like the lesser half of a calling, and he doesn't believe it is. In *Covocational Church Planting*, and earlier in the books he wrote with Lance Ford — *Missional Essentials*, *The Missional Quest*, *Next Door As It Is In Heaven* — the marketplace isn't the thing you do to fund the mission. It's the mission's address.

From inside the North American Mission Board and the Send Network, he's done something unusual: he's carried genuinely uncomfortable ideas into a large denominational structure and made them welcome there. He is the room's bridge between the network and the institution — the person who knows what it costs to move an idea from a conference stage into a system that has to keep working on Monday.

### Alan Hirsch

Alan Hirsch is here as the guest, and the reason is plain enough: a great deal of the vocabulary the others use, he either coined or sharpened. *The Forgotten Ways* made one argument and made it hard to forget — the New Testament church was a movement before it was an institution, and the movement potential never left; it went dormant. His name for that dormant code is *mDNA*. His name for the missing leadership is *APEST* — the five callings of Ephesians 4, three of which (apostle, prophet, evangelist) the Western church quietly exiled. The 5Q work turned that into something a church can actually measure.

He founded the things that the rest of the room keeps brushing up against: Forge, in Melbourne in 1996, with Deb Hirsch and Michael Frost. The 5Q Collective. 100 Movements and the Movement Leaders Collective, both built around a single audacious line — to invest in a hundred Jesus-movements and shift the tracks of history. He is the room's theorist, but the thing to notice is that he doesn't work alone. Nearly every book has a second name on the spine.

### Bree Mills

Bree Mills is the practitioner whose work is happening right now, at ground level, at scale most people don't realize exists. She's an ordained Anglican minister and the Canon of Church Planting for the Diocese of Melbourne, and she's the founding director of Micro Movements — a network now connected to more than five hundred micro churches across Australia and New Zealand. She recently finished a doctorate on how you actually grow innovative missional leaders in the Australian context, which is harder than the American one because Australia is further down the road of forgetting the faith entirely.

Her ideas are concrete in a way that's rare. She'll tell you the exact numbers where a micro church stops working: twenty to twenty-five and you can still disciple people, thirty-five to forty and it wants to multiply, fifty and you've quietly stopped discipling anyone. "When our micro church hit fifty," she says, "I realized I was not discipling the people anymore." She has five markers for what makes a micro church a church and not just a small group, and the fifth one is the one most practitioners drop: partnership with the broader Church. She refuses the isolation that small, fast movements usually fall into. And through The Company, the network she co-founded, she has spent years putting women into church planting where the structures weren't built to let them in.

### JR Woodward

If Hirsch is the room's prophet, JR Woodward is its architect. He takes the big claims and builds the systems that carry them. *Creating a Missional Culture* and *The Church as Movement* (written with Dan White Jr., both carrying Hirsch's foreword) lay out the working parts — the five culture creators, the eight competencies a movement needs, the spaces where belonging actually happens. He runs the V3 church-planting movement and co-founded Missio Alliance and the Praxis Gathering. The frameworks are not decoration; six hundred planters came through V3 in its first decade.

His most demanding book is *The Scandal of Leadership*, and it's the one that gives the room its sharpest edge. Drawing on Walter Wink, René Girard, and William Stringfellow, he argues that leaders fail not mainly because of personal weakness but because they unconsciously copy the systems of domination around them. His line is uncomfortable on purpose: "Not to imitate Christ is to imitate the Powers." He is the person in the room least likely to let a good framework off the hook about power.

### Deb Hirsch

Deb Hirsch co-founded Forge alongside Alan and Michael Frost, and she co-wrote *Untamed* with Alan — but it's a mistake to read her only through the partnership. Her own book, *Redeeming Sex*, went somewhere most Christian leaders won't go on purpose: the honest intersection of sexuality and spirituality, written from years of counseling and social work and from a refusal to treat the marginalized as a category instead of as people at her table. She is the room's voice for the parts of mission that don't fit on a strategy slide — hospitality, the body, the people the institution finds inconvenient. Her recurring theme is *activating female agency*, which is not a slogan but a description of what she's actually done for women coming up behind her.

### Andrew Jones

Andrew Jones — *tallskinnykiwi* to a generation of readers — is the room's chronicler, and the only one whose main body of work is a website. From New Zealand to Western Australia to San Francisco to Prague to the Orkney Islands, he blogged the emerging-church movement in real time for more than twenty years, link by link, while the theorists were still writing the books about it. He thinks in *rhizomes* — church that spreads sideways rather than building upward — and he treated the internet as mission terrain decades before that was a respectable thing to say. He founded BOAZ, worked with the Church Mission Society, and crossed seventy countries doing it.

He's also the honest edge of this exercise, and worth naming as such: his ties to the rest of the room are the least documented. He's a connector, not a systematizer, and connectors leave fewer footnotes than authors do. We'll come back to why that matters.

---

## What's already between them

Read the six together and the lines start drawing themselves.

Start with a city. Forge was born in **Melbourne** in 1996 — Alan Hirsch, Deb Hirsch, Michael Frost. Bree Mills is the Canon of Church Planting for the Diocese of **Melbourne**. The same soil that grew Forge is the soil Mills is planting in now, a generation later, in five hundred micro churches. That's not a coincidence anyone arranged; it's a watershed.

Then follow a single chapter of the Bible. Hirsch built APEST out of Ephesians 4. Woodward took the same five gifts and rebuilt them as his "five culture creators." Brisco carries APEST into NAMB's planter training. Three people, three working systems, one shared root — and each thinks they're solving a slightly different problem.

Then follow the institutions Hirsch founded. **100 Movements** published Woodward's *The Scandal of Leadership*. Woodward sits on the **Movement Leaders Collective**. Bree Mills shows up in that same collective's orbit as a leadership-acceleration catalyst. Hirsch wrote the forewords to two of Woodward's books. *Untamed* has both Hirsches on it. The network is not hypothetical — much of it is already in print.

And then follow the questions that don't have an organization attached, the ones you only see when you read the work side by side:

- Mills knows exactly where discipleship breaks under scale — fifty people. Woodward has spent a career on the architecture that carries a movement past that wall. **They have never, as far as the record shows, compared notes.** One has the field data; the other has the blueprint.
- Brisco's covocational church lives or dies inside denominational systems. Woodward's *Scandal of Leadership* is the most rigorous account anyone in the room has written of how those systems quietly corrupt the people inside them. **One is building in the institution; the other is mapping its undertow.** That's a conversation, not a coincidence.
- Deb Hirsch has spent decades activating female agency in a movement that didn't make room for it. Bree Mills built The Company to do the same thing in the next generation, in another country. **Neither shows up in the other's documented record.** They are running the same play twenty years and ten thousand miles apart.
- Andrew Jones watched and described the emerging-church moment as it happened, while Hirsch and Frost were theorizing it. **The chronicler and the theorists were narrating the same event from two angles** — and the field has mostly kept the books and lost the blog.

None of these are secrets. They're just things no single person in the room has had the time, or the whole-corpus view, to notice all at once. That's the gap. That's what the next part is about.

---

## From six to a hundred

Six people is a number a good facilitator can hold. They can read everyone's books, remember who endorsed whom, and draw the lines by hand over a long weekend. It's work, but it's possible.

A hundred is not possible. A hundred leaders is roughly five thousand pairings — call it forty-nine hundred and fifty — and no facilitator alive can hold five thousand relationships in their head, let alone the contents of two or three hundred books, a few thousand articles, and twenty years of someone's blog. The math is simply past human scale. So at a hundred, the connections stop getting drawn. The work piles up in parallel — five hundred micro churches here, six hundred trained planters there, a theology of the Powers over here, a covocational model over there — and almost none of it gets introduced to the rest.

This is the actual problem Movemental is built to work on, and it's worth being plain about what the tool does and doesn't do.

What it does: it reads the whole body of someone's work and holds it in a form it can compare against everyone else's. Not a summary — the work itself, structured so that Mills's five markers can be set beside Hirsch's mDNA, so that Woodward's eight competencies can be laid over Brisco's paradigm shifts, so that a question one leader asked in 2016 can be matched to the answer another leader published in 2023 without either of them ever meeting. When you do that across a hundred leaders, the model can surface the pairing no human had the bandwidth to see: *these two are solving the same problem from opposite ends; introduce them.* That's the vision — not a directory of who's who, but a standing proposal of who should be working with whom, and why, drawn from what they've actually written.

What it does not do, and the room should hold us to this: it doesn't discern. The model can mistake adjacency for agreement. It will put two leaders next to each other because their words rhyme, when in fact one of them would find the other's project a betrayal of the whole thing. It can read everything Andrew Jones blogged and still miss the relationships he made over coffee that never got written down — which is exactly why his ties looked thin in the research, and exactly the kind of error a room of humans catches in about four seconds. The model proposes. The people in the room decide. A connection the model suggests is a hypothesis, not a verdict, and the most valuable thing this room can do is tell us which of the suggested connections are real and which are just nicely-shaped noise.

So the honest version of the vision is this: the machine can hold the hundred. It cannot judge the hundred. Put the two together — the model's reach and the room's discernment — and you get something neither has alone: a field that can finally see itself.

---

## The whole it belongs to

It would be easy to read all this as flattery, and it isn't meant to be. Every person in this room has also written things that didn't land, run things that didn't last, and held positions they've since revised. The point of reading someone's whole body of work is not to praise it. It's to take it seriously enough to see where it was going — and to notice that it was usually going somewhere alongside other people's work that it never got to meet.

That's the tribute, if it's a tribute at all. Not "you are brilliant." Closer to: *you spent your life saying something true, and you said it next to other people saying true things, and almost nobody ever drew the line between you.* These six have spent decades building in parallel — Melbourne and Kansas City and Los Angeles and the Orkney Islands — and the parallels are not an accident. They're the shape of a single movement that hasn't yet been able to look at itself all at once.

Movemental's bet is that a movement should be able to. That the work of a hundred leaders is more than the sum of a hundred catalogs — that there's a whole there, a connected thing trying to happen, and that the reason it hasn't fully happened is mostly a limit of attention, not of will. Six people in a room is the small version of that whole. A hundred in the field is the real one. The work in front of us is to make the second feel as close, as legible, and as alive as the first.

That's why everyone's here. Not to be seen individually, though they will be. To be seen together — and to find out what the together was for.
